story.heading: Hillary and Bill Clinton Testify in House Epstein Probe, Say They Had No Knowledge of Crimes
story.summary: Former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton and former President Bill Clinton gave separate, closed-door depositions to the House Oversight Committee in Chappaqua, New York, saying they had no knowledge of Jeffrey Epstein's or Ghislaine Maxwell's crimes. The sessions were marked by a temporary pause over a leaked photo, contested procedure, and partisan reactions.

segments.heading: What the Clintons told lawmakers
segments.text: Both Hillary and Bill Clinton, in separate closed-door depositions, told House Oversight Committee members they did not know about Epstein's or Maxwell's criminal activities. Hillary Clinton said she had no idea about their criminal activities and did not recall encountering Jeffrey Epstein. Bill Clinton likewise said his acquaintance with Epstein ended before the crimes came to light and that he never witnessed any indication of wrongdoing during their limited interactions. Each testified for several hours and answered committee questions under oath.

citations.quote: I had no idea about their criminal activities. I do not recall ever encountering Mr. Epstein,
Though my brief acquaintance with Epstein ended years before his crimes came to light

segments.heading: Procedural context and a paused session
segments.text: The Clintons agreed to testify after the Oversight Committee moved to pursue contempt proceedings when earlier offers of sworn statements were declined. The depositions were held in Chappaqua and were conducted behind closed doors; the committee has said it may release video and transcripts. Hillary Clinton's session was paused when Rep. Lauren Boebert sent a photo of her in the room to a social media influencer, which the committee said violated deposition rules. Committee chair Rep. James Comer has said he threatened contempt charges to compel testimony; committee members from both parties questioned the process and sought release of full records.

citations.quote: The deposition was paused after Republican Rep. Lauren Boebert sent a photo of Hillary Clinton in the room to a conservative influencer who posted it on social media, violating the committee's rules for depositions.
No one's accusing, at this moment, the Clintons of any wrongdoing.

segments.heading: Reactions, release plans and partisan framing
segments.text: The depositions prompted calls from the Clintons for greater transparency and for the sessions to be open to reporters; committee leadership has indicated it may release videos and transcripts of the testimony. Republican members of the panel described the process as part of a broader effort to investigate Epstein's connections, while Democrats criticized the committee's approach as partisan. Media accounts differed in tone and detail, with some outlets reporting emotionally charged moments during the sessions and others focusing on procedural developments and statements about the potential release of records.

citations.quote: The incident prompted the former secretary of state to repeat her longstanding demand that the deposition be made open to reporters.
Chairman James Comer, R-Ky., has said that the committee will release video and transcript of their testimonies.

story.heading: Bill Clinton Deposed in House Epstein Probe; Partisan Disputes Over Scope and Evidence
story.summary: Former President Bill Clinton completed a closed-door deposition in the House Oversight Committee's investigation into Jeffrey Epstein, saying in an opening statement that he "saw nothing, and I did nothing wrong." The session, the first time a former president has been compelled to testify to Congress, drew partisan disagreement over evidence and whether President Donald Trump should also be compelled to testify.

segments.heading: Deposition details and Clinton's opening statement
segments.text: Former President Bill Clinton gave a recorded, closed-door deposition in Chappaqua, New York, that lasted more than six hours. In an opening statement he provided to lawmakers and shared publicly, Clinton said he "saw nothing, and I did nothing wrong." He also criticized Republicans for compelling his wife to testify the day before and for subpoenaing family members. Committee materials indicate the deposition is being recorded and the committee expects to release a video and transcript after review.

citations.quote: The closed-door deposition ended after more than six hours of questioning from lawmakers ... "I saw nothing, and I did nothing wrong," the former Democratic president said in an opening statement he shared on social media at the outset of the deposition.
'Before we start, I have to get personal. You made Hillary come in. She had nothing to do with Jeffrey Epstein. Nothing,' Clinton said.

segments.heading: Documentary material cited and Clinton's ties to Epstein
segments.text: Congress and the Department of Justice have released photos and records tied to Epstein that show Clinton with Epstein on a private jet and in other settings. Publicly reported records cited by committee Republicans include counts of Epstein's visits to the Clinton White House and flight logs showing Clinton traveled on Epstein's plane; Clinton has said he flew on Epstein's plane for Foundation-related trips, denies knowledge of Epstein's crimes and denies any wrongdoing. Committee and news reporting also state that neither Bill nor Hillary Clinton have been accused of criminal conduct related to Epstein.

citations.quote: Epstein visited the Clinton White House at least 17 times between 1993 and 1995 and flew at least 27 times on the pedophile's private jet.
Neither Clinton has been accused of any wrongdoing.

segments.heading: Partisan responses and calls regarding President Trump
segments.text: Lawmakers on both sides used the depositions to press competing agendas. Some Democrats renewed calls for President Donald Trump to testify about his ties to Epstein; one Democratic lawmaker said, "We're talking to the wrong president today." Republican Oversight Chairman James Comer told reporters that Clinton said Trump "has never said anything to me to make me think he was involved," a characterization Democrats on the committee disputed and urged be resolved by releasing the full transcript and video of the deposition. Trump publicly expressed that he did not like seeing Clinton deposed and reiterated other comments about related figures named in the records.

citations.quote: Democrats on the House Oversight Committee are renewing calls for President Donald Trump to testify about Jeffrey Epstein ... 'We're talking to the wrong president today,' said Rep. Suhas Subramanyam.
"The president went on to say that the president, Trump, 'has never said anything to me to make me think he was involved,' and he meant with Epstein," Comer said.

"President Clinton did bring up some additional information about discussions with President Trump. I think that the way Chairman Comer described it, I don't think it's a complete, accurate description of what actually was said... So, let's release the full transcript, and you can all get a full record of what actually was said," Rep. Robert Garcia said.

story.heading: U.S. and Israel Carry Out Strikes on Iran; Trump Announces 'Major Combat Operations'
story.summary: The United States and Israel launched strikes on Iran early Saturday, with U.S. President Donald Trump saying the U.S. had begun "major combat operations." Iranian state media and officials reported strikes and retaliatory missile launches, and Iranian authorities cited civilian casualties.

segments.heading: Overview of strikes and official announcements
segments.text: U.S. and Israeli forces announced and carried out attacks on targets in Iran early Saturday. Smoke was reported over Tehran and Iranian media said strikes occurred nationwide, including an apparent first strike near the offices of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. President Trump posted a video saying the United States had begun "major combat operations" aimed at eliminating threats from the Iranian regime.

citations.quote: The United States and Israel launched an attack Saturday on Iran, with the first apparent strike happening near the offices of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
"Our objective is to defend the American people by eliminating imminent threats from the Iranian regime,"

segments.heading: Stated objectives and reported casualties
segments.text: U.S. officials said the operation is intended to degrade Iran's missile and nuclear programs and to protect American personnel and interests. The U.S. president characterized the action as necessary to prevent a nuclear-armed Iran. Iranian state-run media reported civilian casualties, including an account that five students were killed at a girls' school in southern Iran.

citations.quote: He said the military operation would strike Irans missile and nuclear programs as well as its navy.
The strikes have killed five students at a girls school in southern Iran, according to Irans state-run IRNA news agency.

segments.heading: Regional retaliation and wider impacts
segments.text: Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps reported launching missile and drone attacks toward Israel, and other reports said Iran fired missiles toward Israel, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates. At least one civilian death in the UAE was reported from falling debris of intercepted missiles. The developments prompted concerns about broader regional escalation and effects on shipping and oil markets.

citations.quote: Irans Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said they had launched missile and drone attacks toward Israel.
Iran fired retaliatory missiles at Israel, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates capital of Abu Dhabi in response to Trumps announcement, news agencies reported.

story.heading: Kansas law invalidates altered driver’s licenses and birth certificates for transgender residents; two men sue
story.summary: A new Kansas law that requires sex markers on driver’s licenses and birth certificates to match sex assigned at birth took effect Thursday, prompting the state to notify roughly 1,700 people that their credentials are invalid and triggering a lawsuit by two transgender men who say the measure violates state constitutional rights.

segments.heading: What the law does and who it affects
segments.text: The statute bars any sex listing on driver’s licenses and birth certificates other than the one assigned at birth and has resulted in the state notifying people whose records had previously been changed. Officials told affected residents they must obtain new credentials immediately and that no grace period was provided. The number of affected driver’s licenses was reported at roughly 1,700, and the law also invalidated previously changed birth certificates for about 1,800 people.

citations.quote: The law bars any "sex" listing on drivers licenses and birth certificates other than the one assigned at birth and invalidates existing records that donft comply.

"Please note that the Legislature did not include a grace period for updating credentials. That means that once the law is officially enacted, your current credentials will be invalid immediately, and you may be subject to additional penalties if you are operating a vehicle without a valid credential," the Kansas Department of Revenuefs vehicle division said.

segments.heading: Legal challenge filed by two transgender men
segments.text: Two transgender men filed a lawsuit in Douglas County district court the same day the law took effect. They contend the measure violates rights to privacy, personal autonomy and due process under the Kansas Constitution and describe the law as discriminatory. The plaintiffs sought to proceed anonymously and are represented by attorneys from the American Civil Liberties Union.

citations.quote: The men filed their case Thursday, the same day the law took effect, and argue that it violates rights to privacy, personal autonomy and due legal process guaranteed by the Kansas Constitution.
fsThe Kansas Constitution prohibits the Kansas Legislaturefs targeting of transgender individuals for this discriminatory and dehumanizing treatment,fs the lawsuit says.

segments.heading: Enforcement provisions, penalties and public reactions
segments.text: The law includes enforcement mechanisms for compliance with rules about shared single-sex spaces and imposes civil and criminal penalties for violations. Reported provisions include escalating penalties for individuals who enter multiple-occupancy spaces designated for the opposite sex and substantial fines for government entities that do not comply. The measure drew criticism from LGBTQ advocates and Democratic officials, while Republican lawmakers said it provides clarity and protects women and girls.

citations.quote: After a first warning, a second offense could result in a $1,000 civil penalty and a third or subsequent violation is treated as a class B misdemeanor.

Government entities, such as state agencies or local districts, that fail to align with the new regulations are subject to steep fines. Entities face a $25,000 civil penalty for a first violation and $125,000 for each subsequent violation.
"It's all happening very quickly, and my heart is broken for anyone who this is directly impacting," LGBTQ activist Jae Moyer told the Star.
"When I go home, people believe there are just two sexes, male and female," Blasi said.

story.heading: Nearly blind refugee in Buffalo found dead days after being dropped off by Border Patrol
story.summary: Nurul Amin Shah Alam, a 56-year-old refugee from Myanmar, was released from Erie County custody on Feb. 19 and dropped off by U.S. Border Patrol at a Tim Hortons in Buffalo. Surveillance video and statements from authorities show he walked away from the site; five days later he was found dead downtown. Authorities are investigating the circumstances of his release and death.

segments.heading: Release and last known movements
segments.text: After his release from Erie County custody on Feb. 19, federal Border Patrol agents picked up Nurul Amin Shah Alam and dropped him at a Tim Hortons in Buffalo. Surveillance footage obtained by local reporters shows Shah Alam leaving the Border Patrol vehicle and walking past the restaurant. Officials and advocates say his family was not told where he had been released and his lawyer reported him missing several days later. Buffalo police later located his body downtown.

citations.quote: the footage it obtained shows Shah Alam being let out of the Border Patrol van, which can be seen driving away, then walking by the restaurant’s locked front door.

"Border Patrol agents offered him a courtesy ride, which he chose to accept to a coffee shop, determined to be a warm, safe location near his last known address, rather than be released directly from the Border Patrol station," the agency said.

segments.heading: Prior arrest, charges and legal status
segments.text: Shah Alam had been arrested about a year earlier after an incident in which officers said he injured two Buffalo police officers during his arrest. Body-camera footage released by police shows officers confronting Shah Alam in a snowy yard; police say he resisted and bit officers. After his arrest a federal immigration detainer was issued. Advocates say Shah Alam, who spoke little English and had limited vision, may have wandered into the wrong property. He agreed to a plea deal in February and was released on bail this month; the Erie County district attorney's office said it was not notified of his release until after his death.

citations.quote: In the body camera videos, one officer says "I�27m going to shoot you, dude" before firing Tasers and then taking him to the ground in a struggle and cuffing his hands behind his back. Police say Shah Alam bit two officers.

"At the time of the plea, it was our understanding and agreement with defense counsel that he would remain in custody on bail while pending sentence. Our office was not aware of his release until yesterday," District Attorney Michael J. Keane said in the statement to MS NOW on Thursday.

segments.heading: Investigation, medical findings and public response
segments.text: Shah Alam was found dead on a downtown sidewalk five days after the Tim Hortons drop-off. Authorities are reviewing surveillance footage to trace his movements. Officials have said differing things about the cause of death: the Buffalo Police Department told reporters the medical examiner had concluded the death was "health related" and ruled out exposure or homicide, while the Erie County Department of Health later said no determination had been made. Local television reporting cited by one outlet said investigators reported natural causes. City leaders and advocates have called for answers; Buffalo's mayor criticized the decision to leave a nearly blind, non-English-speaking man at the site, and the family has said it wants justice.

citations.quote: The Buffalo Police Department told reporters that the medical examiner had concluded that the death was "health related" and ruled out exposure or homicide, but the Erie County Department of Health later disputed that account, saying no determination had been made.
WGRZ reported that investigators said Shah Alam died from natural causes.

story.heading: Sweden says it disrupted a suspected Russian drone near French carrier in Malmö
story.summary: Swedish forces say they detected and disrupted a suspected drone launched from a nearby Russian ship while the French aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle was docked in Malmö for NATO exercises. Swedish, French and Russian officials have issued differing statements about the incident and its origin.

segments.heading: Detection and countermeasures
segments.text: Swedish naval forces reported observing a drone take off from a nearby Russian signals-intelligence vessel in the Öresund Strait and said they took measures that caused the drone to lose contact. French officials involved in the carrier security arrangements said the carrier's protection system remained effective and that the event did not affect operations.

citations.quote: a Swedish naval vessel detected a drone taking off from a Russian signals intelligence ship in the Öresund strait
The Swedish armed forces jammed a suspected Russian drone Feb. 25 as it approached a French nuclear-powered aircraft carrier while docked in Malmö
"This system showed it is robust, and this event had no impact on the activity of the aircraft carrier battle group,"

segments.heading: Official statements and divergent accounts
segments.text: Swedish authorities described the incident as a breach of access regulations and a violation of Swedish airspace and said a Russian vessel, the Zhigulevsk, was in Swedish waters at the time. Swedish leaders indicated the drone likely came from the Russian ship. The Kremlin disputed that attribution, calling it unreasonable to assume the drone was Russian solely because a Russian ship was nearby.

citations.quote: "The incident constitutes a breach of Sweden's access regulations and a violation of Swedish airspace," Swedish Defense Minister Pål Jonson wrote in a social media post.
Asked what country he thinks the drone belongs to, he replied: "Probably Russia."
its quite absurd to claim that the drone was Russian just because a Russian ship was nearby.

segments.heading: Context and regional concerns
segments.text: The Charles de Gaulle was visiting Malmö as part of NATO-related exercises, and officials said the ship's security arrangements included integration with Swedish forces. The incident has been discussed against a broader backdrop of Western officials warning about alleged Russian attempts at sabotage and disruption in Europe since 2022.

citations.quote: The French nuclear-powered aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle is in the southern Swedish city of Malmö this week as part of regular NATO exercise activities.
The drills brought NATO naval forces into the Baltic Sea region at a time of rising tensions with Moscow.
Western officials say Russia is masterminding a campaign of sabotage and disruption across Europe.

story.heading: World Economic Forum President Børge Brende Resigns After Scrutiny Over Ties to Jeffrey Epstein
story.summary: Børge Brende said he is stepping down as president and CEO of the World Economic Forum following scrutiny of his contacts with the late Jeffrey Epstein. An independent review reportedly found no additional concerns beyond what had already been disclosed; the forum named an interim leader as it manages the leadership transition.

segments.heading: Resignation announced
segments.text: Børge Brende, who served as president and chief executive of the World Economic Forum, said he decided to step down "after careful consideration." He framed the move as intended to allow the forum to continue its work without distractions. The forum publicly announced the departure and framed it as a leadership change taking place amid an ongoing internal review.

citations.quote: said in a statement that he had decided "after careful consideration" to step down as president and chief executive of the forum

"I am grateful for the incredible collaboration with my colleagues, partners, and constituents, and I believe now is the right moment for the Forum to continue its important work without distractions," Brende said in a statement.

segments.heading: Contacts with Jeffrey Epstein and review findings
segments.text: Documents and reporting prompted the WEF to open a review into Brende's relationship with Jeffrey Epstein after records indicated they had dined together and exchanged messages. Some reports cited U.S. Justice Department disclosures saying Brende attended multiple business dinners and exchanged communications with Epstein. WEF co-chairs said an independent review conducted by outside counsel concluded and that the findings identified "no additional concerns beyond what has been previously disclosed."

citations.quote: the WEF announced earlier this month that it was opening an internal review into Brende to determine his relationship with Epstein after files indicated the two had dined together several times and exchanged messages.
Disclosures by the U.S. Justice Department indicated that Brende had attended three business dinners with Epstein and engaged him in communications through email and texts, according to Reuters.
The independent review conducted by outside counsel has concluded. The findings stated that there were no additional concerns beyond what has been previously disclosed.
attended dinners at the creep's Manhattan home in 2018 and 2019.

segments.heading: Brende's statements and interim leadership
segments.text: Brende has said he cooperated with the investigation and characterized his meetings with Epstein as business-related, adding that he was unaware of Epstein's criminal history. The WEF's governing leadership named Alois Zwinggi as interim president and CEO while the board oversees the search for a permanent successor.

citations.quote: Brende told Norwegian broadcaster NRK at the time that he was cooperating with the investigation, that he only met Epstein in business settings and that he had been unaware of Epstein's criminal background.
"I was completely unaware of Epstein's past and criminal activities," Brende noted, according to the outlet.
They said that Alois Zwinggi will serve as the forum's interim president and CEO.
"We respect his decision to step down," they said.

story.heading: Minnesota governor unveils anti-fraud proposals after federal pause of Medicaid payments
story.summary: The Biden-era CMS administrator and the Trump administration announced a temporary deferral of $259.5 million in Medicaid payments to Minnesota and gave the state 60 days to propose corrective actions. Gov. Tim Walz criticized the move and presented legislation intended to strengthen fraud detection and oversight as officials and prosecutors offer differing estimates of the scope of alleged fraud.

segments.heading: Federal deferral of Medicaid payments and federal demands
segments.text: Federal officials said they would defer roughly $259 million in Medicaid payments to Minnesota and set conditions for restoring those funds. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services said the payments would be released only after the state submitted a comprehensive corrective action plan, and federal officials gave Minnesota a 60-day window to respond.

citations.quote: the government would hold off on paying $259.5 million to Minnesota for Medicaid
And he gave Walz 60 days to respond.

We are stopping the federal payments that will go to the state government until the state government takes its obligations seriously to stop the fraud that's being perpetrated against the American taxpayer.

segments.heading: Governor Walz's response and proposed anti-fraud legislation
segments.text: Governor Tim Walz criticized the federal action as politically motivated and announced a package of legislation aimed at improving fraud detection, oversight, investigative authority and penalties. The proposals include creating a centralized Office of the Inspector General to lead fraud prevention efforts; state leaders continue to debate whether that office should have law enforcement authority or focus on investigations while leaving enforcement to existing state agencies.

story.heading: Federal Judge Allows White House Ballroom Construction to Proceed, Leaves Door Open for Revised Legal Challenge
story.summary: A federal judge declined to halt construction of a privately funded 90,000-square-foot ballroom on the site of the former East Wing of the White House, finding the preservation group's initial lawsuit unlikely to succeed on the legal theory presented. The judge invited the plaintiffs to amend their complaint, and other federal review panels will continue to consider the project.

segments.heading: Court ruling and legal reasoning
segments.text: U.S. District Judge Richard Leon rejected the National Trust for Historic Preservation's request for a preliminary injunction, finding the group was unlikely to succeed on the legal theory it advanced. Leon said the complaint focused on the President's constitutional authority rather than the statutory authority the administration invoked and noted that the office overseeing the project is not an "agency" under the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), which affected the suit's viability. He indicated the plaintiffs could amend their complaint and that he would consider such an amendment promptly.

citations.quote: U.S. District Judge Richard Leon ruled that the National Trust for Historic Preservation was unlikely to succeed on the merits of its bid to temporarily halt President Donald Trump's project.
The construction project is being managed by the White House Office of the Executive Agency, which Leon said is not a federal agency that can be sued under federal administrative law

segments.heading: Project details, funding and timeline
segments.text: The White House has described the planned ballroom as a privately funded, 90,000-square-foot facility that the administration says will seat up to 999 people. The construction follows demolition of the East Wing and is being paid for by private donations, including from the president. Federal advisory panels have been or will be asked to review the plans; the Commission of Fine Arts approved the design and the National Capital Planning Commission is scheduled to discuss the project on March 5.

citations.quote: The White House said private donations, including from Trump himself, would pay for the planned construction of a 90,000-square-foot (8,400-square-meter) ballroom.
The donors include corporations such as Apple, Amazon, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, Google, Ripple and Coinbase, as well as individuals like the family of Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick.

segments.heading: Reactions and next steps
segments.text: President Trump celebrated the ruling on social media and characterized it as a victory for the project. Preservationists said they were disappointed by the lack of an injunction but noted the court found they have standing and encouraged amendment of their complaint. The judge also previously cautioned the administration against taking irreversible steps on underground work that could dictate the scope of future construction above ground. The case may proceed if the preservation group files an amended complaint under a different legal theory.

citations.quote: Trump used his social media account to hail the ruling as "Great news for America."

We are also pleased that he encouraged us to amend our complaint  specifically, to assert that the president has acted beyond his statutory authority  and we plan to do so promptly," Quillen said in a statement.
But the door remains open for further challenges. If the historic trust amends the lawsuit, Leon said, the judge would "expeditiously consider it."

story.heading: Columbia student detained by ICE at campus housing released after mayor's meeting with President Trump
story.summary: A Columbia University student was detained early Thursday by federal immigration agents at university housing amid allegations they misrepresented their identity to gain entry. New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani told social media he raised the case with President Trump during a White House meeting, and the student was released hours later. The Department of Homeland Security says the student's visa had been terminated and she was placed in removal proceedings but released pending a hearing.

segments.heading: Detention at campus housing and university response
segments.text: Federal agents detained a Columbia University student at a residential building on the Morningside Heights campus early Thursday. Columbia's acting president, Claire Shipman, said agents gained entry by representing themselves as police searching for a missing child and that security cameras captured agents showing pictures in a hallway. The student posted on social media saying she had been arrested by DHS. University officials said they were working to obtain more details and expressed concern about the manner in which agents accessed the building.

citations.quote: The agents gained entry by stating they were police searching for a missing child," Shipman said in a video released Thursday night.

Once inside the apartment, it became clear they had misrepresented themselves. A public safety officer arrived, asked multiple times for a warrant, which was not produced, and asked for time to call his boss, which was not given.

segments.heading: Mayor's meeting with the president and the student's release
segments.text: Mayor Zohran Mamdani said he raised concerns about the student's detention during an unscheduled White House meeting with President Trump and that the president agreed to release her. Mamdani posted on social media following the meeting that Trump informed him the student "will be released imminently." Columbia later said the student was released from detainment and the university expressed relief.

citations.quote: In a social media post Thursday afternoon, Mayor Zohran Mamdani said he expressed concerns about the arrest during an unrelated meeting with Trump, who then agreed to release her immediately.
Just got off the phone with President Trump. In our meeting earlier, I shared my concerns about Columbia student Elaina Aghayeva... He just informed me that she will be released imminently.

segments.heading: Immigration status, official statements and legal context
segments.text: Department of Homeland Security officials said the student's student visa had been terminated in 2016 for failing to attend classes and that she was placed in removal proceedings and released while awaiting a hearing. DHS also said building residents let officers into the apartment and that agents identified themselves. University officials and some local elected leaders criticized what they described as misrepresentation by agents and emphasized that law enforcement must follow legal processes to access non-public university areas. Commentators have noted that some forms of disguise or misrepresentation by immigration authorities have been used elsewhere and are generally legal in many cases.

citations.quote: A Department of Homeland Security spokesperson said Aghayeva's student visa had been terminated in 2016 for failing to attend classes.

story.heading: Federal prosecutors decline to appeal ruling that bars death penalty in Luigi Mangione case
story.summary: Federal prosecutors said they will not appeal a judge’s ruling that removed a death-penalty-eligible federal murder charge against Luigi Mangione, clearing the way for a federal trial in September while a separate state murder trial remains scheduled for June. The judge found the federal murder count legally flawed and left stalking charges that carry a maximum sentence of life in prison.

segments.heading: Prosecutors will not seek appellate review; trial timing
segments.text: Deputy U.S. prosecutors informed the court they will not ask the federal appeals court to reverse U.S. District Judge Margaret Garnett’s decision that foreclosed capital punishment in the federal case. That development allows the federal trial to proceed in the fall, while a state murder trial remains scheduled for June.

citations.quote: the government will not ask the 2nd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals to reverse her decision, clearing the way for a trial beginning in September. His state murder trial is set to start in June.
Luigi Mangione has escaped the death penalty.

segments.heading: Judge Garnett’s ruling and legal reasoning
segments.text: Judge Garnett dismissed the specific federal murder charge that had allowed prosecutors to seek the death penalty, concluding the charge was legally flawed. Her ruling focused on whether the government had shown Mangione killed Thompson while committing another qualifying "crime of violence"; she concluded that the stalking-related charges retained in the federal case did not meet that definition under relevant precedent and noted the decision may appear counterintuitive but was grounded in Supreme Court guidance and controlling law.

citations.quote: Garnett last month dismissed a federal murder charge — murder through use of a firearm — that had enabled prosecutors to seek capital punishment, finding it legally flawed.

'Over the course of the last two decades or so, the Supreme Court has embarked upon a legal journey, explained herein, that now requires lower courts to engage in an analysis totally divorced from the conduct at issue and centered on the hypothetically least serious conduct that the charged crime could possibly cover,' she wrote.

segments.heading: Case facts, charges and defendant status
segments.text: Brian Thompson was shot and killed on Dec. 4, 2024, in Manhattan; surveillance video showed a masked gunman firing from behind and police reported messages written on ammunition recovered in the case. Mangione, 27, has pleaded not guilty in both the federal and state cases. The federal indictment no longer includes a charge that would permit capital punishment but retains stalking counts that carry a possible life sentence; a separate state murder trial remains scheduled for June.

citations.quote: Thompson, 50, was killed on Dec. 4, 2024, as he walked to a midtown Manhattan hotel ... Surveillance video showed a masked gunman shooting him from behind. Police say 'delay,' 'deny' and 'depose' were written on the ammunition.
Mangione also faces life in prison at a separate murder trial in state court set for June.

story.heading: Man arrested on DUI outside Nancy Guthrie's home; police say arrest not tied to disappearance
story.summary: A 34-year-old man was detained on misdemeanor DUI charges after being stopped near the Tucson home of missing 84-year-old Nancy Guthrie. Authorities said the arrest was not related to the ongoing investigation into her disappearance.

segments.heading: Arrest and charges
segments.text: Pima County deputies detained 34-year-old Antonio De Jesus Pena-Campos outside the Tucson residence where Nancy Guthrie was last seen. Officials said he was arrested on misdemeanor driving-under-the-influence charges after failing a field sobriety test and was taken into custody. The sheriff's department stated the arrest is not related to the investigation into Guthrie's disappearance.

segments.heading: Reported behavior near the home
segments.text: Reporters and media at the scene described the driver as moving slowly near a roadside memorial and stopping repeatedly. A NewsNation correspondent and other media reported the vehicle passed the property multiple times, and a photographer said the driver appeared to be looking at a photo of Nancy Guthrie on his phone. Deputies were recorded using a flashlight on the vehicle and conducting field sobriety exercises with the driver.

citations.quote: NewsNation correspondent Brian Entin reported that the driver 'just kept stopping' outside the property, passing between '50 and 100 times.'

segments.heading: Context of the Guthrie investigation
segments.text: Nancy Guthrie, 84, was reported missing after she did not attend a church service following a January 31 dinner and game night at her daughter's home. Investigators have said her pacemaker last synced with her iPhone around 2:30 a.m. on the night she was last seen. Security footage from a doorbell camera captured an unidentified person in a ski mask near her door early on February 1. Media reports additionally said the FBI scaled back its on-site activity and moved a command post farther from Phoenix as the search continued.

story.heading: Savannah Guthrie family offers $1 million reward as investigation into mother Nancy Guthrie's disappearance continues
story.summary: Savannah Guthrie announced a family reward of up to $1 million for information leading to the whereabouts of her 84-year-old mother, Nancy Guthrie, as investigators from local and federal agencies continue to examine evidence including surveillance footage and DNA. Authorities say the case is being treated as a crime; no arrests have been reported.

segments.heading: Family reward, public plea and donations
segments.text: Savannah Guthrie posted video appeals and announced a family reward of up to $1 million for information that leads to her mother’s recovery. The family also said it is making a separate donation to a national child protection organization. Guthrie acknowledged in public statements that her mother may be dead while continuing to ask anyone with information to come forward.

segments.heading: Evidence and investigative activity
segments.text: Law enforcement treating the disappearance as a possible crime have reviewed surveillance footage showing a masked person at the Guthrie residence and have found blood at the scene. DNA recovered from a glove found a few miles from the home has been analyzed by investigators; officials reported that the glove DNA produced no match in the national CODIS database and did not match DNA found at the house. Federal and local agencies, including the FBI and the Pima County Sheriff's Office, are involved; authorities say family members are not suspects and no arrests have been announced.

story.heading: Pakistan and Afghanistan Exchange Strikes as Pakistan's Defence Minister Declares 'Open War'
story.summary: A series of cross-border attacks and airstrikes between Pakistan and Afghanistan escalated this week, prompting Pakistan's defence minister to say the country is in "open war" with Afghanistan. Both sides have issued conflicting casualty and territorial claims, and international actors have called for restraint.

segments.heading: Escalation and official declarations
segments.text: Pakistan carried out airstrikes inside Afghanistan in response to an incident Islamabad described as an Afghan cross-border attack. Pakistan's Defence Minister Khawaja Mohammad Asif posted that "our patience has now run out" and characterised the situation as "open war." The Taliban and Afghan officials said they launched retaliatory operations and that strikes hit locations including Kabul, Kandahar and Paktia provinces. Both sides describe ongoing military actions along sections of the Durand Line and the border.

citations.quote: "Our patience has now run out. Now it is open war between us,"
The clashes came after the Taliban said it launched retaliatory strikes on Pakistani military positions, while Islamabad said it was responding to unprovoked fire in the area.

segments.heading: Conflicting casualty and territorial claims
segments.text: The parties have published differing accounts of casualties and territory seized. Afghanistan's defence ministry reported substantially higher Pakistani losses and said it had captured some Pakistani personnel and positions. Pakistani officials have rejected those accounts and issued their own figures for Taliban losses and destroyed positions. Independent verification of the competing claims was not reported in the sources.

citations.quote: Afghanistans Defense Ministry said overnight that 55 Pakistani soldiers had been killed, including some whose bodies were taken into Afghanistan, and that "several others were captured alive." It said eight Afghan soldiers were killed and 11 wounded.
Mosharraf Ali Zaidi, the prime minister's spokesman, claimed that at least 133 Afghan fighters were killed and more than 200 wounded, with 27 Afghani posts destroyed and nine fighters captured.
Reuters said it could not independently verify those claims.

segments.heading: Context, accusations and international reaction
segments.text: The spike in violence follows months of tension and earlier strikes; sources note that a ceasefire mediated by Qatar and Turkey had been in place after prior clashes. Islamabad has accused the Afghan Taliban of sheltering militant groups responsible for attacks in Pakistan; the Afghan side denies those claims and says its operations are retaliatory. The UN secretary-general urged both sides to protect civilians and pursue diplomacy.

citations.quote: The escalation comes months after Qatar and Turkey mediated a ceasefire between the two sides.
The fighting follows Pakistans accusations that the Taliban is sheltering TTP militants behind a surge in violence and suicide attacks.

story.heading: Avalanche near Lake Tahoe kills multiple backcountry skiers; separate rescue at Palisades Tahoe recorded
story.summary: An avalanche on February 17 near Castle Peak in the Lake Tahoe region buried a group of backcountry skiers and resulted in multiple deaths, while the following day two skiers at Palisades Tahoe dug a man out of deep snow. Reports vary on the exact casualty count; local authorities and rescuers have urged caution and proper safety equipment in deep-snow conditions.

segments.heading: Castle Peak avalanche and casualty reports
segments.text: An avalanche on February 17 struck a group of backcountry skiers near Castle Peak (Boreal Mountain Resort) in the Lake Tahoe area. Sources report that several members of the group were killed and others were rescued; the precise number of fatalities reported varies across outlets. Authorities shifted the operation from rescue to recovery as bodies were located and families were notified.

story.summary: Actor Eric Dane, best known as Dr. Mark Sloan on Grey's Anatomy, died at 53 after a publicly disclosed diagnosis of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). story.heading: Astronaut Mike Fincke Identifies Himself as Crew-11 Member Who Experienced Medical Event Aboard ISS
story.summary: NASA confirmed that astronaut Mike Fincke experienced a medical event aboard the International Space Station on Jan. 7 that led to Crew-11 returning to Earth on Jan. 15. Fincke said his condition stabilized on board, the return was coordinated to access advanced imaging on Earth, and he is recovering at NASA’s Johnson Space Center.

segments.heading: Incident and early return
segments.text: NASA and the astronaut Mike Fincke reported that Fincke experienced a medical event on Jan. 7 while aboard the International Space Station. After evaluation, the agency determined an early return was the safest option; Crew-11 splashed down off the coast of San Diego on Jan. 15. Some news accounts described the situation as prompting the agency's first medical evacuation or the first time a crew on the ISS had their mission ended early for medical reasons.

citations.quote: On Jan. 7, while aboard the International Space Station, I experienced a medical event that required immediate attention from my incredible crewmates.

After further evaluation, NASA determined the safest course was an early return for Crew-11 1 not an emergency, but a carefully coordinated plan to be able to take advantage of advanced medical imaging not available on the space station. On Jan. 15, we splashed down off the coast of San Diego after an amazing five-and-a-half-month mission,
NASAs Mike Fincke identified himself Wednesday as the astronaut whose medical condition prompted the space agencys first medical evacuation.

segments.heading: On-board medical response and diagnostics
segments.text: Fincke and his crewmates used on-board diagnostic tools during the event, and NASA arranged the early return so Fincke could access imaging capabilities not available on the station. Fincke said a portable ultrasound aboard the ISS was particularly useful during the incident; the station has carried a modified ultrasound unit in recent years for biomedical use and health checks.

citations.quote: 'Having a portable ultrasound machine helped us in this situation; we were able to take a look at things that we didn't have,'

NASA determined the safest course was an early return for Crew-11 1 not an emergency, but a carefully coordinated plan to be able to take advantage of advanced medical imaging not available on the space station.
The ISS has carried a modified off-the-shelf ultrasound machine called Ultrasound 2 since 2011, which is used in both biomedical research and routine health checkups.

segments.heading: Recovery and official comments
segments.text: Fincke said his condition stabilized with the assistance of crewmates and flight surgeons and that he is doing well while undergoing standard post-flight reconditioning at NASA's Johnson Space Center in Houston. He expressed gratitude to his fellow crew members, medical teams and supporting organizations for their response and care. He declined to disclose specific medical details publicly.

citations.quote: "I am deeply grateful to my fellow Expedition 74 members ... as well as the entire NASA team, SpaceX, and the medical professionals at Scripps Memorial Hospital La Jolla near San Diego. Their professionalism and dedication ensured a positive outcome,"
He did not say what was wrong with him but explained that his condition quickly stabilized thanks to his crewmates and flight surgeons on the ground.
"I'm doing very well and continuing standard post-flight reconditioning at NASA's Johnson Space Center in Houston."

story.heading: Companies Seek Refunds After Supreme Court Strikes Down Trump-Era Tariffs
story.summary: Major and smaller U.S. companies have filed suits seeking refunds after the Supreme Court ruled that former President Trump lacked authority to impose broad global tariffs under IEEPA. Businesses and legal advocates are pursuing cases in the U.S. Court of International Trade while disputes over repayment procedures and the scale of claims continue.

segments.heading: Court decision and immediate corporate actions
segments.text: The Supreme Court ruled that the tariffs imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) were unlawful. In the days after the ruling, some companies moved to reclaim duties; FedEx filed a complaint in the U.S. Court of International Trade seeking a full refund of duties it paid under the tariff program.

citations.quote: the Supreme Court struck down the tariffs, ruling that Trump did not have the authority to impose sweeping global duties under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, known as IEEPA.

segments.heading: Reported financial impacts on firms
segments.text: Companies report a range of financial effects from the tariffs. FedEx has reported a large single-year tariff charge, while smaller importers say levies materially reduced earnings or constrained operations. Some small firms have already begun litigation seeking repayment of amounts they say harmed cash flow and profitability.

citations.quote: FedEx said it paid $1 billion in tariffs in 2025, sparking a 16% hit to its earnings.
Basic Fun 12 the company that makes Tonka Trucks, Lite Brite and Care Bears 13 said $7 million in tariffs last year tanked its projected earnings by 65%.

segments.heading: Legal process ahead and scale of claims
segments.text: Most refund claims are expected to be handled in the U.S. Court of International Trade, where multiple related cases are pending. Advocacy groups and affected companies have filed motions and lawsuits to obtain refunds; analyses and filings indicate thousands of importers have sought recovery, and courts will consider procedural questions such as consolidation and the timing of repayment.

story.heading: Trump Sets 10–15 Day Deadline for Iran Nuclear Talks as U.S. Military Forces Build in Region
story.summary: President Donald Trump has given Iran roughly 10 to 15 days to reach terms in nuclear negotiations, saying the timeline is backed by military force. U.S. naval and air assets have been redeployed to the region as diplomatic talks continue in Geneva and Iranian officials respond publicly amid domestic unrest.

segments.heading: U.S. deadline and military posture
segments.text: President Trump has publicly set a short timeline—about 10 to 15 days—for Iran to accept U.S. terms in ongoing nuclear negotiations and has repeatedly linked that timeline to the potential use of force. The administration has reinforced that diplomatic pressure with an increased military presence in the region, including carrier strike groups and additional aircraft, as talks proceed in Geneva.

citations.quote: "I would think that would be enough time  10, 15 days, pretty much maximum," Trump said, warning that absent an agreement, "it's going to be unfortunate for them."

segments.heading: Diplomatic negotiations and U.S. demands
segments.text: U.S. envoys met with Iranian representatives in Geneva as negotiators exchanged proposals. The administration has described core demands that it says should be part of any agreement, while Iranian officials have at points characterized progress differently. The parties have discussed continued talks and the exchange of draft proposals, but sources reported disagreement on key issues including enrichment limits, missile programs, and Iran's support for proxy groups.

segments.heading: Iranian response and regional dynamics

segments.heading: U.S. domestic debate and potential legal checks
segments.text: The administration's public deadline and military moves have prompted discussion in Washington about Congress's role in authorizing force. Members of both parties have signaled different approaches, with some preparing a War Powers resolution to limit unilateral military action and others expressing support for a stronger response to Tehran.

story.heading: Armed Intruder Fatally Shot Near Mar‑a‑Lago; FBI Is Investigating
story.summary: A 21‑year‑old man identified as Austin Tucker Martin was shot and killed after entering a secure perimeter at former President Donald Trump’s Mar‑a‑Lago resort early Sunday, Palm Beach authorities and the Secret Service said. Officials say the man carried a shotgun and a fuel can, was ordered to drop the items and raised the weapon before agents and a deputy fired. The FBI has taken the lead on the investigation.

segments.heading: What happened at Mar‑a‑Lago
segments.text: Palm Beach law enforcement and U.S. Secret Service agents encountered a man near the north gate of Mar‑a‑Lago at about 1:30 a.m. local time. Authorities said the individual was carrying a shotgun and a fuel can; he was ordered to drop both items, put down the fuel can and then raised the shotgun. A Palm Beach County sheriff's deputy and two Secret Service agents fired and the man was pronounced dead at the scene. No one under Secret Service protection was at the property at the time.

citations.quote: A security detail detected the man near the north gate of President Donald Trump's Mar-a-Lago resort in Palm Beach, Florida, at about 1:30 a.m. local time. ... Palm Beach County Sheriff Ric Bradshaw alleged that Martin was carrying a shotgun and a fuel can, and the officials ordered him to drop the items. Martin put the gas can down, but the sheriff said he "raised the shotgun to a shooting position", and authorities fired their weapons at him in response.

The man, whom the FBI later identified as Austin Tucker Martin, 21, of Moore County, North Carolina, was shot at by Secret Service agents and a Palm Beach County Sheriff’s deputy, the Secret Service said. The agency said he appeared to be carrying a shotgun and a fuel can near the resort’s front gate.

segments.heading: Identity of the deceased and reported background
segments.text: Authorities identified the man as 21‑year‑old Austin Tucker Martin of Moore County, North Carolina; local reports said he had been reported missing in the days before the incident. Media reporting about his background varied: some local reporting described family tragedies and included a text message that investigators and media have noted in initial reporting, while law enforcement agencies said the FBI is examining Martin’s background and potential motives.

citations.quote: The Palm Beach County Sheriff's Office identified the man as Austin Tucker Martin, 21, from North Carolina. Martin was reported missing within the last few days, Reuters reported.

A text message uncovered by TMZ shows that Martin may have been motivated by the release of files related to Jeffrey Epstein. 'I don't know if you read up on the Epstein Files, but evil is real and unmistakable,' Martin texted on Feb 15.

segments.heading: Investigation, official statements and local requests

story.heading: Willie Colón, pioneering salsa trombonist and composer, dies aged 75
story.summary: Willie Colón, a leading figure in salsa music known for his work as a trombonist, vocalist and composer, died aged 75, his family said. The family announced his passing and asked for privacy; the cause of death was not disclosed. Colón recorded influential albums, collaborated with Héctor Lavoe and Rubén Blades, and received multiple industry honors during a career that spanned decades.

segments.heading: Life and career
segments.text: Willie Colón was born in the Bronx to Puerto Rican parents and became a central figure in the development of salsa. He signed with Fania Records as a teenager and released his first album, El Malo, in the late 1960s. He worked closely with singers such as Héctor Lavoe and later with Rubén Blades; their collaborations produced genre-defining recordings, including the album Siembra. Colón's music incorporated elements of jazz, rock and traditional Caribbean rhythms and helped broaden the reach of salsa internationally.

segments.heading: Death and family statement

story.heading: Supreme Court Rejects Trumps Use of Emergency Law for Global Tariffs; Administration Moves to Reimpose Levies
story.summary: The US Supreme Court ruled that former President Donald Trump exceeded his authority by imposing broad global tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. The decision could reduce consumer tariff burdens, but the administration has moved to impose new tariffs under different statutory authority, leaving economic effects and timing uncertain.

segments.heading: Court ruling and legal reasoning
segments.text: The Supreme Court held that the president exceeded his authority when he used the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose broad global tariffs. The courts majority pointed to the statutes text and the absence of an express grant of tariff powers, and the decision invalidated the tariffs that had been enacted under IEEPA.

citations.quote: The US Supreme Court on Friday struck down President Trumps tariffs in a 6-3 decision.
Absent from this lengthy list of powers is any mention of tariffs or duties.
The Supreme Court dealt a huge blow to President Donald Trump on Friday, ruling that the sweeping global tariffs he imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act are unconstitutional.

segments.heading: Potential effects for consumers and businesses
segments.text: Analysts and economists cited in reporting said the ruling could reduce the tariff-related portion of consumer prices, especially for goods with high import content or intense price competition. Estimates referenced in reporting suggested an average households tariff burden could fall materially if tariffs are not reimposed by other means, while previously observed price increases in some categories may be partially reversed over time. Reporters and economists also warned that some effects already transmitted through prices could persist.

citations.quote: The Supreme Court's landmark ruling striking down Donald Trump's sweeping global tariffs could lead to significant savings for Americans.
The Supreme Court's decision could slash the average household's tariff burden roughly by half this year to between $600 and $800, according to the Yale Budget Lab.
Furniture and bedding prices have risen four percent during that time period, while dishes and flatware increased by five percent.
'We could see, over the coming months, some reversals of tariffependent price increases we saw last year,' Michael Pearce, the chief US economist at Oxford Economics, told USA Today.
Ultimately, even if Trumps backup tariff plans fail as well, the effects his tariffs have already had will take a long time to abate, and some could be permanent.

segments.heading: Administration response and alternative measures
segments.text: Within hours of the ruling, the administration announced measures intended to restore tariffs under different statutory authority. Reporting states the president signed an executive order to impose a temporary import surcharge and cited Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 as the legal basis for new measures that would allow short-term tariffs of up to 15 percent. The order included a list of exemptions and set an effective date for the new surcharge, but how broadly and for how long new levies will apply remains contingent on implementation and potential further legal or political challenges.

citations.quote: President Trump on Friday followed through on his earlier promise to sign an executive order imposing a 10% global tariff on every country importing goods into the United States.
The new tariffs fall under his authority under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, which allows him to impose tariffs of up to 15% for 150 days with few limitations.
Still, how much consumers ultimately save will depend on the Trump administration's next steps.

story.heading: Trump Announces Higher Global Tariffs as Court Limits Emergency Authority; Implementation and Reactions Vary
story.summary: Following the Supreme Court's decision limiting the use of emergency powers to impose tariffs, President Donald Trump announced an increase in a temporary global tariff to 15%. Federal notices and agency actions show a 10% rate being applied in practice, and lawmakers, businesses and foreign leaders responded with criticism, legal questions and concern about market effects.

segments.heading: Announcement and legal context
segments.text: After the Supreme Court ruled that he exceeded his authority under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), President Trump announced on social media that he would raise a newly invoked global tariff from 10% to 15%, citing an alternative statutory authority. The court decision narrowed the administration's use of IEEPA for tariffs and prompted the administration to point to other trade statutes as a basis for temporary duties.

citations.quote: I, as President of the United States of America, will be, effective immediately, raising the 10% Worldwide Tariff on Countries ... to the fully allowed, and legally tested, 15% level,

The court on Friday ruled that Trump exceeded his authority with the way he imposed tariffs on trading partners using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), a law from 1977 reserved for national emergencies.

segments.heading: Implementation and legal mechanics
segments.text: Officials and agency notices have shown differences between the president's public announcement and the tariff rate applied at the border. U.S. Customs and Border Protection published a notice implementing an additional ad valorem rate of 10% for imports while the administration has pointed to Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 as allowing temporary duties of up to 15% for a limited period (150 days) pending congressional action.

citations.quote: all imports would "be subject to an additional ad valorem rate of 10%,"
the law according to section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 ... allows the president to impose a levy of up to 15% for 150 days,

segments.heading: Reactions, potential impacts and unresolved questions
segments.text: The announcements and implementation choices drew criticism from congressional Democrats and some business groups, while some analysts and conservative commentators supported using other trade authorities. Business associations and state attorneys general have sought refunds tied to previously collected emergency tariffs, and foreign leaders warned that tariff uncertainty harms trade. Observers also noted uncertainty about refunds to importers and which rate will prevail if administrations list different levels.

story.heading: U.S. and Israel Conduct Strikes on Iran; Targets and Responses Reported
story.summary: U.S. and Israeli forces carried out coordinated strikes in Iran early Saturday, with reporting indicating attacks near the Supreme Leader's compound and on military and intelligence sites. Leaders from the U.S. and Israel described the action as aimed at eliminating threats, and Iran and regional actors reported retaliatory launches and alerts.

segments.heading: Strikes reported and identified targets
segments.text: Multiple accounts say the initial strikes struck areas of Tehran, including the compound associated with Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. U.S. and Israeli officials and briefed persons told reporters the operation targeted elements of Iran's military and intelligence infrastructure, and that strikes were intended to degrade ballistic missile supplies and other capabilities.

citations.quote: The first strikes of the attack appeared to target the compound home to Irans 86-year-old Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in downtown Tehran.

the Israeli military is targeting Irans leadership... Other targets of the operation include Irans ballistic missile supplies, a stockpile which has long threatened Israel as well as U.S. bases in the Middle East, and intelligence targets, including the Iranian Ministry of Intelligence

segments.heading: Official statements and stated objectives
segments.text: U.S. and Israeli leaders framed the action as defensive and aimed at eliminating threats posed by Iran's regime and its capabilities. In a public message, U.S. President Donald Trump described the operation as intended to defend the American people and urged Iranians to seize control of their government after the strikes. Israeli officials described the strikes as pre-emptive measures to remove threats to the state.

citations.quote: When we are finished, take over your government. It will be yours to take.
The State of Israel launched a pre-emptive attack against Iran to remove threats to the State of Israel, Defence Minister Israel Katz said.

segments.heading: Regional responses and immediate effects
segments.text: Iranian forces and regional actors reported immediate responses and alerts. Iran's Revolutionary Guard said it launched a "first wave" of drones and missiles toward Israel, and officials in Bahrain reported a missile attack near the U.S. Navy 5th Fleet headquarters there. Several countries closed or restricted airspace and civil defense sirens were reported in multiple states.

citations.quote: Irans paramilitary Revolutionary Guard said it responded by launching a "first wave" of drones and missiles targeting Israel
Bahrain said that a missile attack targeted the U.S. Navys 5th Fleet headquarters in the island kingdom.

story.heading: Artemis II fueling tests uncover issues; March launch window removed
story.summary: NASA initially targeted an early March launch for the crewed Artemis II mission after a fueling test, but subsequent ground data showed a propulsion-system issue that prompted preparations to roll the Space Launch System rocket back to the Vehicle Assembly Building and removed the March launch window. Engineers are diagnosing hydrogen- and helium-related problems while the agency determines the next available launch opportunities.

segments.heading: Recent schedule updates and crew preparations
segments.text: After a fueling test at Kennedy Space Center, NASA publicly identified March 6 as an initial target date for Artemis II and the agency’s communications indicated the mission team was preparing for final launch activities. The four assigned astronauts had entered pre-flight quarantine as part of launch preparations before the new technical issue emerged.

citations.quote: NASA announced that it successfully conducted a fueling test of the powerful SLS rocket, and with that, it’s aiming to launch the Artemis II mission in two weeks, on March 6.

Artemis II commander Reid Wiseman, pilot Victor Glover, mission specialist Christina Koch and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen are expected to enter pre-flight medical quarantine Friday evening at the Johnson Space Center in Houston.

segments.heading: Technical issues identified: hydrogen and helium
segments.text: During pre-launch ground tests engineers encountered propellant-related problems. Earlier in February, a wet dress rehearsal was halted after controllers detected leaking super‑cooled liquid hydrogen at the pad. Overnight data later showed an interruption in helium flow into the rocket’s interim cryogenic propulsion stage; the helium anomaly and prior hydrogen seepage are being investigated as separate but related ground-support and vehicle-flow issues. Similar hydrogen-related issues were noted during preparations for the uncrewed Artemis I mission.

citations.quote: launch controllers found that enough super-chilled liquid hydrogen fuel was leaking at the launchpad to prompt safety concerns.
overnight data showed an interruption in the flow of helium into the SLS's interim cryogenic propulsion stage.
A similar issue was also observed during preparation for Artemis I.

segments.heading: Next steps and schedule implications
segments.text: NASA announced it would prepare to roll the SLS rocket back to the Vehicle Assembly Building to allow hands‑on inspections and corrective work, a step that removes the March launch window from consideration. Agency statements and reporting indicate the next available launch opportunity opens in April. Teams will continue diagnostic work on the propulsion‑stage helium flow and on hydrogen systems before confirming a revised launch date.

citations.quote: "We will begin preparations for rollback, and this will take the March launch window out of consideration," NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman wrote on X.
The available April launch dates include the 1st through the 6th of the month.
